did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning architectures, specifically an InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble, a PatchNet–ResNet50 ensemble, a Vision Transformer (ViT), and a MaxViT–Hybrid model, to perform automated classification of seven classes of dermoscopic skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative study of four deep learning architectures (InceptionV4–DenseNet201, PatchNet–ResNet50, ViT, and MaxViT–Hybrid) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (Model Architecture, Methodology), reports quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, F1-score, precision, recall), and discusses new findings regarding model performance, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Deep Learning Architectures for Skin Lesion Classification: A Comparative Study of CNN, Transformer and Hybrid Models
journal: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
brief_summary: This study presents a comparative evaluation of four deep learning architectures for classifying seven classes of dermoscopic skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The models assessed include an InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble, a PatchNet–ResNet50 ensemble, a Vision Transformer (ViT), and a MaxViT–Hybrid model. Performance was measured using precision, recall, F1-score, and overall accuracy to address the challenges of imbalanced multi-class classification. The InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble achieved the highest overall accuracy (0.91) and weighted-average metrics, while the MaxViT–Hybrid model attained the highest macro-averaged F1-score (0.85) and balanced performance across challenging minority classes. The results indicate that hybrid architectures combining convolutional and transformer-based features offer superior operational balance and reliability for automated skin lesion diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset; identified as a challenging minority class.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of dermoscopic skin lesions (AKIEC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, NV, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. Models predict discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma) using softmax probability distributions. Performance is evaluated using precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: The HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 images) dataset consists of dermoscopic images collected over nearly two decades from the Medical University of Vienna and the Queensland Skin Cancer Clinic. It contains seven clinically or histopathologically validated skin lesion categories.

use.training: Used for training the four deep learning architectures (InceptionV4–DenseNet201, PatchNet–ResNet50, ViT, MaxViT–Hybrid) with 80% of the dataset allocated for training.
use.validation: Used for validation with 10% of the dataset (half of the remaining 20% after training split) allocated via stratified sampling.
use.testing: Used for testing with 10% of the dataset (half of the remaining 20% after training split) allocated via stratified sampling.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper relies on comparative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance tests.

HAM10000.metric: Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Accuracy, Macro-Averaged Metrics, Weighted-Averaged Metrics
HAM10000.notes: InceptionV4–DenseNet201 achieved the highest overall accuracy (0.91) and weighted-average metrics. MaxViT–Hybrid achieved the highest macro-averaged F1-score (0.85) and macro-averaged precision (0.86). ViT had the worst macro-average recall (0.67) and macro-average F1-score (0.69). MaxViT–Hybrid showed balanced performance across minority classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC).

architecture_used.main_model: Four architectures evaluated: InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble, PatchNet–ResNet50 ensemble, Vision Transformer (ViT), and MaxViT–Hybrid.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the specific models in this study, though related work mentions ImageNet-pretrained CNNs.
architecture_used.input_size: InceptionV4: 299 × 299 × 3; MaxViT–Hybrid: 224 × 224 × 3; ViT: patches of size 16 × 16; PatchNet–ResNet50: patch-based (specific input size not explicitly stated, but validation/test images resized to 224 × 224).

InceptionV4–DenseNet201.InceptionV4_details: Stem block, four Inception-A modules (35 × 35), seven Inception-B modules (17 × 17), three Inception-C modules (8 × 8), Reduction-A and Reduction-B layers. No residual connections. Global average pooling, fully connected layer, dropout (keep rate 0.8), softmax.
InceptionV4–DenseNet201.DenseNet201_details: 201-layer CNN with dense blocks. Each layer receives feature maps of all preceding layers. Uses bottleneck layers with 1 × 1 and 3 × 3 convolutions.

PatchNet–ResNet50.PatchNet_details: Context-restricted CNN operating on small image patches. Patch-level attention mechanism. Concatenated into a single 192-dimensional representation, passed through a fully connected layer with 128 ReLU units, dropout, and final classification layer.

Vision Transformer (ViT).details: Divides image into 16 × 16 patches, flattened and embedded with learnable positional encodings. 12 identical transformer encoder layers with multi-head self-attention and feedforward networks. Layer normalization and residual connections.

MaxViT–Hybrid.details: Convolutional stem with four MaxViT encoder stages downsampling feature maps from 56 × 56 to 7 × 7. Custom metadata classification head with two fully connected layers (512 and 256 units), Batch Normalization, SiLU activation, and Dropout. Multi-axis attention mechanisms.

training_details.data_augmentation: Random resized cropping and horizontal flipping during training.
training_details.preprocessing: Images resized and normalized using ImageNet mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]. Validation/test images resized to 256 pixels on shorter side and center-cropped to 224 × 224.

classification_or_output_head.InceptionV4–DenseNet201: Seven-dimensional logit outputs from both networks concatenated and passed through a fusion layer for joint learning, followed by softmax.
classification_or_output_head.PatchNet–ResNet50: Final classification layer after 128 ReLU units and dropout.
classification_or_output_head.ViT: Standard transformer classification head (implied by 'final classification layer' in context of ViT description).
classification_or_output_head.MaxViT–Hybrid: Softmax layer producing probability distribution across seven diagnostic classes.

preprocessing_techniques: Parsing of HAM10000 dataset metadata to map diagnostic labels (dx) to integer class identifiers and generate file paths
Stratified sampling for dataset splitting (80% training, 10% validation, 10% testing) to maintain class distributions
Resizing images to 256 pixels on the shorter side for validation and testing
Center-cropping validation and testing images to 224 × 224
Normalization using ImageNet mean [0.485, 0.456, 0.406] and standard deviation [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]
Random resized cropping for data augmentation during training
Horizontal flipping for data augmentation during training
Semi-automated preprocessing of HAM10000 images including filtering, cropping, colour normalization, and standardization (prior to model training)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase robustness and reduce overfitting.
Applied during training to increase robustness and reduce overfitting.

conclusions: Hybrid architectures combining convolutional and transformer-based features offer superior operational balance and reliability for automated skin lesion diagnosis compared to non-hybrid transformer-only architectures.

The MaxViT–Hybrid model achieved the highest macro-averaged F1-score (0.85) and macro-averaged precision (0.86), demonstrating consistent performance across both majority and minority classes (AKIEC, DF, VASC).

The InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble achieved the highest overall accuracy (0.91) and weighted-average metrics, indicating strong performance on frequently occurring classes but an inherent bias toward the populated NV class.

The MaxViT–Hybrid model's superior balanced performance is attributed to its multi-axis attention mechanism, which captures both local textural patterns and global morphological structures, and its hierarchical design which retains spatial detail.

The Vision Transformer (ViT) exhibited the worst macro-average recall (0.67) and F1-score (0.69), indicating difficulties in generalizing across all lesion types, particularly on minority classes like MEL and BCC, due to a lack of inherent spatial inductive biases.
PatchNet–ResNet50 showed strong precision for DF and VASC but struggled with global context, resulting in lower recall for MEL (0.57).
Real-time clinical applications of these models may be hindered by hardware constraints and computational expenses.

The results have not been clinically validated and should not be considered direct evidence for diagnosis; future work should incorporate medically relevant information such as biopsy analysis and patient history.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which has known class imbalance issues (e.g., NV is the most populated class), potentially biasing models toward majority classes despite the use of stratified sampling.

The results have not been clinically validated and should not be considered direct evidence for diagnosis; the authors explicitly state that future work should incorporate medically relevant information such as biopsy analysis and patient history.
Real-time clinical applications may be hindered by hardware constraints and computational expenses associated with the evaluated deep learning architectures.
The Vision Transformer (ViT) exhibited poor generalization on minority classes (MEL recall 0.38, BCC recall 0.53) due to a lack of inherent spatial inductive biases found in CNNs.

The InceptionV4–DenseNet201 ensemble showed an inherent bias toward the populated NV class, achieving high weighted-average metrics (0.91) but lower macro-averaged F1-score (0.84) compared to the MaxViT–Hybrid model.
No statistical significance testing was performed; conclusions are based solely on comparative performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).
PatchNet–ResNet50 struggled with global context, resulting in low recall for MEL (0.57), highlighting limitations of patch-based analysis for lesions requiring broader morphological interpretation.
